  10. @Malfunction-Junction: 7:35 Bigger Diesel engines will run forever at idle or just above. However the newer smaller more efficient diesel engines are being designed and developed to run at hotter temperatures. Y’all’s new boat will the Volvo D6. I wouldn’t recommend running that engine anywhere under 1,200rpm underway. In the generator world you want 30% engine load bare minimum. 75-90% load continuous during operation is how to make a diesel last the longest. Just slowly trotting around genuinely reduces engine lifetime. They need to be worked.

  17. @QU1RKONE: If a generator burns 5–10 liters (1.3–2.6 gallons) per hour, and your speed reduction adds 24 hours to your passage, you’ve just added 120–240 liters of fuel burn to your trip total. 

Additional costs to consider for longer passages:
* maintenance for the additional wear and tear of the 
* provisions and operational supplies
* crew costs

When calculating fuel economy for an explorer yacht, looking only at the main engines is a mistake. "Slow steaming" to save fuel often triggers hidden costs that can outweigh the savings:

  2. @DisciplinePelajar: Monaco, Billionaire Playground 😎
  3. @high-reshallucinations: 1. Russian oligarch yachts
After the Ukraine war sanctions, many Russian-owned yachts were seized in places like Spain, Italy, France, and the US. Some owners moved their yachts to Turkey because Turkey did not join Western sanctions.

2. Superyachts are symbols of extreme wealth
The film shows huge yachts with luxury cabins, crews, security, submarines, private events, and weekly rental prices around €500,000.

3. The environmental criticism
A major theme is that superyachts burn massive amounts of diesel. The transcript says some consume around 500 liters per hour, making them controversial because of their carbon footprint.

4. Life behind the luxury
The documentary contrasts guest luxury with crew life. Guests have huge suites, cinemas, decks, and private lounges, while crew members live in small shared cabins and work under strict standards.

5. New luxury markets
Saudi Arabia, Dubai, Hong Kong, and Thailand are shown as growing yacht hubs. Saudi Arabia wants places like Jeddah to become major luxury boating destinations.

6. “Green” yachts
A big part focuses on Francis Lapp and Sunreef in Poland, building solar/electric catamarans. Their yacht Marie-Joseph uses solar panels integrated into the hull and electric motors, aiming to reduce diesel use.

7. The contradiction
The documentary’s title suggests “Russian oligarch yachts,” but much of it is actually about the wider yacht industry: luxury, sales, chartering, crew life, Saudi/Dubai markets, and eco-yacht technology.

Overall: it’s less a pure investigation into Russian oligarchs and more a documentary about how the superyacht industry is trying to survive bad publicity by moving into new markets and promoting “eco-luxury.”

No expense will be spared, from the genuine&nbsp; Formula 1 cars owner can race around the deck, to&nbsp;&nbsp; the swarovski crystal-studded jacuzzi, champagne&nbsp; bar and helicopter landing pad. Advanced dynamic&nbsp;&nbsp; positioning and GPS hybrid drive systems will&nbsp; enable her to remain stationary without anchors. Slated for unveiling in 2024, this&nbsp; billion-dollar vessel aims to redefine&nbsp;&nbsp; the limits of what is possible in&nbsp; a privately owned superyacht. For&nbsp;&nbsp; the ultra-wealthy designers of Streets&nbsp; of Monaco, only their imaginations set&nbsp;&nbsp; boundaries. She will undoubtedly turn&nbsp; heads wherever she eventually sails. Number 2 - Eclipse Occupying the number two spot is the grand&nbsp;&nbsp; superyacht Eclipse, owned by Russian billionaire&nbsp; and industrial tycoon Roman Abramovich.&nbsp;&nbsp; Constructed in 2010 by the esteemed German&nbsp; shipyard Blohm + Voss, Eclipse measures over&nbsp;&nbsp; 550 feet long and reportedly cost Mr. Abramovich&nbsp; $1.5 billion, more than the GDP of some nations! Yes, no expense was spared designing Eclipse&nbsp; as the ultimate personal luxury liner. Some&nbsp;&nbsp; highlights include 24 elegantly appointed&nbsp; guest cabins that can welcome over 90 crew&nbsp;&nbsp; and visitors across vast deck spaces and saloons.&nbsp; She also features two swimming pools, hot tubs,&nbsp;&nbsp; a private cinema, beauty salon, spa, and of&nbsp; course, a helicopter hangar and landing pad. The nine-deck master suite spans two entire&nbsp; levels and is completely bulletproof,&nbsp;&nbsp; with armored plating and panels throughout.&nbsp; Eclipse was also the first private yacht to&nbsp;&nbsp; install an intruder detection system&nbsp; with missile countermeasures! She can&nbsp;&nbsp; accommodate parties of nearly 100&nbsp; thanks to enormous interior volume. Powered by four MTU diesel engines, Eclipse&nbsp; reaches over 20 knots despite her tremendous&nbsp;&nbsp; size and weight. Yes, she provides Roman&nbsp; Abramovich with his own portable private&nbsp;&nbsp; island able to spirit him away in secrecy&nbsp; and safety to any ocean destination. Number 1 - History Supreme Though not the largest in size,&nbsp;&nbsp; her lavish use of precious metals throughout make&nbsp; her the priciest private yacht ever sold at an&nbsp;&nbsp; astonishing $4.8 billion dollars! Conceived and&nbsp; constructed by UK luxury designer Stuart Hughes,&nbsp;&nbsp; she was built for an anonymous Malaysian&nbsp; businessman who clearly valued exclusivity. Let's consider what exactly $4 billion can&nbsp; buy. The entire base of History Supreme is&nbsp;&nbsp; formed from 10,000 kilograms of&nbsp; solid gold. The master bedroom&nbsp;&nbsp; includes wall art made from meteoric&nbsp; stone and genuine dinosaur bone! A&nbsp;&nbsp; platinum coated Monopoly board with diamond&nbsp; playing pieces also graces the interior. Throughout her spaces, surfaces are coated&nbsp; with precious metals - 100 million-year-old&nbsp;&nbsp; Northumberland platinum, meteorite slices,&nbsp; gold flakes and accents. Even mundane&nbsp;&nbsp; features like door hinges, tap handles and&nbsp; stunt rails are fashioned from platinum,&nbsp;&nbsp; gold and silver. No detail or material was&nbsp; too precious for use somewhere onboard. Without question, no private yacht rivals the&nbsp; sheer decadence and value of History Supreme.&nbsp;&nbsp; She fulfills the elite notion that anything can be&nbsp; built, regardless of cost, if one simply wills it.&nbsp;&nbsp; Her sheer extravagance has cemented her place as&nbsp; the undisputed champion of luxury vessels. Yes,&nbsp;&nbsp; she is a golden symbol representing mankind's&nbsp; limitless appetite for ever greater opulence.

incredibly thorough so every day the entire boat is vacuumed and dusted and wiped every morning when the guests get up there's a full room service and then throughout the day say if the toilet gets used or if the guests lie on the bed the stewardesses will be straight back in there folding the toilet paper again ironing the bed sheets making everything look like no one has ever been in there care of all the guests linen their sheets their towels and think about the crew or the crew uniform or the crew personal clothes cruise sheets you know tea towels all of my rags from the galley and are off this is where the interior team get a chance to really deep clean the vessel so they are quite literally taking earbuds and toothpicks and cleaning with that so they will go right into the their inventories so they take care of all the wine the spirits on board all the drinks you know soft drinks water that is all part of the interior team and they also take care of the crew areas so guaranteed i mean it depends on every vessel but typically you are not guaranteed weekends specifically if the guests are on board if you have got a six-week charter you're going to be working every anyway when the guests aren't on board that's when the hours get much more reasonable you're working from eight to five you more often than not will have weekends off and you can really enjoy the area - The “Yachting” Trap: Why Most People Who Buy Yachts Are Broke Within 3 Years: Kind: captions Language: en A used 58-ft sportfish boat sells in North Carolina. The sale price is $1 million. The seller walks away having paid $100,000 in broker commissions he never knew were fixed. His buyer's broker, the one he thought was working for him, collected $50,000 of that paid by the seller and never disclosed it. That was the cheap part of the deal. The closing check was smaller than the first year's operating bill. This video is about the financial structure behind that moment and why it repeats itself on marina docks from Florida to the Mediterranean every single season. The yachting trap is not one thing. It is a layered system with five moving parts and every part is designed to profit from someone who does not know the math. Who actually gets rich in the yachting world and is it ever the owner? Polished hulls gleam beneath showroom lights. Brokers in pressed blazers glide between espresso bars and leather sofas speaking in the practice language of possibility. The pitch is never about the boat itself. It is about a new identity. Here, the yacht is a passport to freedom, to status, to a kind of belonging that only exists dockside. The buyer, often successful in every other arena, steps into this world with a mix of calculation and longing. The brochures promise more than a vessel. They promise transformation. Words like investment, equity, and legacy drift through the conversation, but the real hook is always lifestyle. Brokers talk about Mediterranean summers, long weekends in the Bahamas, the silent glide out of the marina at sunrise. They mention family memories, business deals sealed on the off deck, friendships cemented over sunset cocktails. There is an unspoken suggestion that this is a club with velvet ropes and the only ticket is the signature at the bottom of the contract. For a moment, the buyer can almost see it. The gentle sway at anchor, the applause of water against the hull, the quiet certainty of having arrived. The pen hovers over the dotted line. The numbers, what they mean and what they hide, are for later. For now, the dream is real and the only thing left is to step aboard. The numbers the showroom never mentions are the ones that matter most. First, the baseline. Annual operating costs run between 10 and 15% of the yacht's original purchase price. That means for a $500,000 boat, the owner should expect to spend 50 to 75,000 dollars every single year just to keep the vessel afloat, insured, fueled, and maintained. [music] No exotic breakdowns, no storms, just the routine bill for showing up at the dock. Next, usage. The US Coast Guard's most recent survey puts average recreational boat [music] use at about 55 days per year. That's less than eight weeks out of 52. Most owners get on the water for a handful of weekends and a single extended trip, if that. The rest of the year, the yacht sits idle accumulating costs while losing value. Then comes depreciation. Industry-wide data show a new yacht loses between 10 and 20% of its value in the first year alone. By the fifth year, the typical vessel has shed 40 to 50% of its original price. This is not an outlier, it's the expected curve regardless of brand or maintenance. For a $500,000 boat, that is $250,000 gone before the first serious refit. These three benchmarks, 10 to 15% in annual costs, 55 days of use, and up to half the value lost in 5 years, form the backbone of the real ownership equation. They are not worst-case scenarios, but the industry's own consensus drawn from broker guides and Coast Guard data. The dream is sold in broad strokes, but the ledger is written in fine print. Charter programs are often pitched as the rational antidote to yachting's notorious cash burn. The promise is simple. Put your boat to work, let paying guests cover the bills, and enjoy the best of both worlds. The reality is governed by thresholds that most owners never cross. According to Vital Charters internal modeling, the break-even point for a typical 60-ft motor yacht lands between 16 and 20 weeks of paid charter per year. Anything less and the owner is subsidizing every charter guest out of pocket. The national average for private use, about 55 days a year, falls well short of this threshold and few owners have the appetite or network to fill a third of the calendar with paying clients. Stretch the lens to a decade. A $3 million yacht run through a standard charter management program generates a total outflow of roughly $8.1 million over 10 years. That figure absorbs purchase price, depreciation, operating costs, management fees, and the periodic refits that come with heavy use. The number is not a worst-case scenario. It is the sum of routine invoices, insurance renewals, and the relentless march of marina rate hikes. Charter income, even at a healthy utilization rate, rarely claws back more than a fraction of this drain. The illusion comes from gross revenue. Charter brochures spotlight the top line. $30,000 a week booked solid for the season. But after management commissions, unscheduled repairs, and the high wear cost curve of commercial use, the net is a shadow of the headline figure. Meanwhile, the same $3 million placed in a broad market index fund would have doubled over the same period compounding quietly while the yacht's value eroded. The math is not subtle. Over a decade, the real contest is not between yachting and doing nothing. It is between the dream of ownership and the opportunity cost of capital. The numbers do not just favor the market, they expose the lifestyle pitch as a system designed to extract, not to build, wealth for the typical buyer. A $500,000 yacht purchase, mapped line by line, reveals the real mechanics of ownership. The initial outlay is rarely just the down payment. With a standard 25% down, the buyer wires $125,000 to the dealer on day one. The remaining $375,000 is financed, usually at 5% to 6% interest over 5 to 10 years. Year one begins with a silent deduction, depreciation. Industry data puts the first year loss at 15% of the original price. $75,000 erased before the first season ends. The boat is now worth $425,000 on paper, but the loan principal remains untouched. Operating expenses arrive monthly. The 10% rule, accepted by every major brokerage, means $50,000 vanishes over 12 months for dockage, insurance, fuel, maintenance, and the steady drip of marina fees. These are not luxury upgrades or emergencies, just the baseline for keeping the vessel ready. Add annual interest on the loan, which at 5% means roughly $19,000 in year one alone. The running total, $75,000 lost to depreciation, $50,000 spent on operations, $19,000 paid in interest, and $125,000 gone in the initial down payment. The first 12 months consumed $269,000 of cash and equity. Year two repeats the cycle. Another $50,000 for operations, another $18,000 in the loan balance declines, and an additional 12% depreciation, about $51,000, shrinks the boat's value further. By the end of year three, the owner faces a cumulative outflow approaching $400,000, not including the original purchase The boat itself, now three years older, has lost up to 40% of its value trading at $300,000 to $320,000 on the open market. A looming cost sits just over the horizon. Major refits, engines, hull, electronics arrive around the five-year mark often demanding another 10% to 20% of the original price. For many, the decision to sell comes just before this grenade goes off, locking in losses and passing the next round of expenses to the next owner. The ledger does not lie. The system extracts value from the buyer at every step and the numbers compound quietly year after year. Brokers occupy a unique position in the yachting ecosystem. Their income is tied directly to the deal. A standard 10% commission is split evenly between the listing broker and the buyer's broker. On a $1 million sale, that is $100,000, $50,000 to each side. The seller pays the commission, but the buyer's broker is presented as their advocate. In reality, both brokers are incentivized to close the transaction, not to challenge its wisdom or interrogate the numbers. The commission is not a bonus for exceptional service. It is a built-in cost baked into every contract regardless of whether the buyer sails away with a sound investment or a rapidly depreciating asset. This arrangement is no secret within the industry, but it is rarely spelled out for buyers. The International Yacht Brokers Association, or IYBA, which sets the tone for much of the US market, has historically defended the 10% split as fair compensation for expertise and deal management. Yet, the lack of transparency has led to legal scrutiny. In 2023, the Lamb versus IYBA antitrust case landed in federal court. The claim was that IYBA members conspired to fix commissions at a flat $50,000 on $1 million transactions. Internal communications cited in the complaint described the fee as fixed [music] and non-negotiable, a direct challenge to the idea of open competition. The case remains unresolved, but its existence confirms that these commission structures are not just tradition. They are protected by industry consensus and at times enforced through opaque agreements. For buyers, this means the person guiding them through the process is paid by the other side and rewarded for closing quickly, not for ensuring long-term value. The advice chain is only as honest as the incentives allow. The moment the contract is signed and the commission is wired, any alignment between buyer and broker dissolves. The system is not accidental. It is engineered to move product, not to build wealth for the person stepping onto the dock for the first time. Referrals and incentives shape every step of yacht ownership. When a buyer asks for a marine survey, the broker's recommendation often comes with quiet expectations. Surveyors who keep deals moving forward are favored, while those who flag too many issues risk being left out of future business. The inspection, paid for by the buyer, becomes less about uncovering problems and more about protecting the sale. Loyalty flows through the broker's network, not to the owner's interests. Charter management follows the same logic. The promise is that chartering will offset costs, but the contract tells a different story. Management fees, locked in at 15 to 20% of gross charter revenue, are deducted before the owner sees a dollar. For every $100,000 earned, $15,000 to $20,000 is claimed by the management company, regardless of whether the owner turns a profit. The contract spells this out, but it is rarely the focus in sales pitches. >> [music] >> The manager's priority is bookings, not the owner's bottom line. Marina contracts add another layer. Annual escalation clauses, often buried in the fine print, quietly raise berth fees by 3 to 5% each year. A $1,500 monthly slip can reach nearly $1,700 by the third year. These increases compound, are rarely discussed up front, and only become clear with the next invoice. Escalation is rarely explained at the point of sale. Tax deductions are dangled as a final incentive. The so-called second home deduction is tightly limited. The yacht must qualify as a residence, >> be used for more than 14 days, and at least 10% of rental days, and survive IRS scrutiny. Most owners do not qualify, but the myth persists at the point of sale. Deduction. Language is used to close deals, not to guarantee savings. Each mechanism, survey referral, charter fee, marina escalation, tax myth, ensures service providers get paid first. The owner absorbs the risk and rising costs. Owner A stepped into the marina with a plan that looked solid on paper. The purchase price was $600,000. The down payment was $150,000. The rest was $450,000 financed at 6% interest over 10 years. The first season brought the expected joys, family weekends, a handful of business outings, the sense of having arrived. But by the end of year one, the numbers had already started to slip out of reach. The boat's value dropped by $90,000, 15%, before the first annual service was even due. Operating costs came in right on cue at $60,000, matching the 10% rule quoted by every brokerage. Loan interest alone added $27,000 to the tally. In 12 months, $177,000 was gone, and the boat's market value had slipped to $510,000. Year two, the pattern repeated. Another $60,000 in operating costs, another $25,000 in interest, and resale value fell by another 12%, $61,000 evaporated from the resale value. By the end of year three, the boat was worth $360,000. The loan balance stood at $415,000. Selling meant cutting a check just to close out the debt. The owner's equity had been hollowed out by a system that front-loads loss and back-loads risk. This is not a cautionary tale about poor planning. It is the statistical median for financed buyers who enter at retail and exit before the first major refit. Owner B believed in the charter offset. The pitch was simple. Buy an $850,000 catamaran, sign with a management company, and let paying guests cover the bills. Year one delivered $62,000 in gross charter revenue. Management fees, set at 20%, took $12,400 off the top. Heavy charter use drove up maintenance, $28,000 spent on repairs, and accelerated wear. That left $21,600 in net income against $127,000 in total ownership costs for the year. The ledger did not lie. The charter program covered less than a fifth of the actual outflow. By year two, bookings dropped, the charter firm terminated the contract, and the owner was left with a high-hour boat, mounting expenses, and no path to break even. The promise of neutralizing costs through chartering [music] had failed, not because of a bad season, but because the math was never in the owner's favor. Both owners started with different strategies, one with a classic loan, the other with a charter plan. Both ended up in the same place, chasing sunk costs, boxed in by a system that rewards everyone except the person holding the keys. The numbers are not outliers, they are the rule, disguised by glossy marketing and the language of opportunity. Owner C walked into the marina with no illusions about financing or fast returns. This time, there was no bank involved, just a wire transfer for the full price of a lightly used sloop, $220,000 paid in cash. On paper, it looked like a safe harbor. No loan, no interest, no risk of being underwater. But the map was still wrong. The first year brought an unexpected $32,000 in deferred maintenance. The survey had missed corroded rigging and a leaking fuel tank, issues quietly overlooked in the rush to close. The owner paid out of pocket, chalking it up to bad luck, but the pattern was already set. Insurance, which had seemed like a simple checkbox, turned into a moving target. By year two, the underwriter flagged the hull's age and refused renewal without costly upgrades. The only replacement policy available came with a $15,000 annual premium, more than double the original quote. The marina, meanwhile, had its own escalation clause. The slip fee, $700 a month at signing, rose to $1,000 by the third year, a 43% jump triggered by a clause buried on page six of the contract. The owner, now locked into the berth, watched fixed costs outpace any savings from buying with cash. A three-way cross-section tells the story. Owner A, boxed in by loan payments and a shrinking asset. Owner B, squeezed by charter math and management fees. Owner C, supposedly immune, still drained by hidden maintenance, insurance hikes, and rising marina costs. The specifics differ, but the destination is the same. Whether the entry point is debt, charter, or cash, the system's incentives ensure that costs compound quietly and persistently, while value erodes. The only constant across all profiles is that the service providers, brokers, surveyors, managers, marina operators, get paid first. The owner, regardless of strategy, absorbs the risk and the loss. The pattern is not accidental. It is engineered by contracts, clauses, and a network of incentives that operate in plain sight. The promise of yachting as a rational asset dissolves when the numbers are laid side by side. What remains is a lifestyle product, sold as an investment, but structured so that the owner is always the last to get paid. Today, the illusion persists. Glossy brochures still frame yachts as assets, not consumables. The real math, depreciation rates exceeding 20% per year, operating costs rivaling mortgage payments, remains buried in fine print. In a market engineered for extraction, clarity is power. The only real windfall belongs to those who read the ledger, not just the dream. The map was never neutral. Now you know where it leads.

- Inside a $10,000 Luxury Yacht Cruise | Billionaire Lifestyle 😱: Kind: captions Language: en Inside a $10,000 per night luxury yacht cruise, Billionaire Lifestyle at Sea. What if I told you that spending $10,000 a night doesn't just buy luxury, it buys a completely different world, one with its own rules? This isn't just a yacht. It's a floating billionaire playground. But there are things you're absolutely not allowed to do on board. All right, so imagine this. You step onto a massive luxury yacht. The crew greets you by name. There's a private chef, infinity deck, jacuzzi, maybe even a helipad. Everything screams wealth. But here's the thing nobody talks about. This level of luxury comes with strict rules. Some of them are surprising, some are weird, and some can actually get you kicked off the yacht. Let's break down the 10 things you're not allowed to do on a $10,000 per night luxury yacht. 10. No shoes on deck. Yeah, sounds simple, right? But this rule is serious. Luxury yachts use high-end teak wood flooring, and even the tiniest scratch can cost thousands of dollars to repair. That's why guests are either barefoot or given special soft slippers. So, yeah, your expensive sneakers, they're staying off. Nine, no red wine indoors. This one surprises most people. Red wine is often restricted or even banned inside certain areas of the yacht. Why? Because one spill on white leather furniture or carpets can ruin surfaces that cost more than a car. So, if you're sipping wine, it's usually in controlled areas only. Eight. No unapproved guests. You can't just invite friends over like it's a house party. Every guest on board must be preapproved and documented. Why? Because of security, insurance, and international maritime laws. Yachts operate in international waters, so things are much stricter than normal property rules. Seven, no smoking inside. This one is obvious, but still strictly enforced. Smoking indoors is completely banned due to fire hazards, expensive interiors, air filtration systems. Some yachts even restrict smoking to a single designated outdoor area. Six. No loud parties after certain hours. to Cersei. It's a yacht. Yes, it's luxury, but it's not always a 24/7 party. Many yachts have quiet hour policies, especially when anchored near coastal areas. Also, crew members need rest, too. And they're running a million-doll operation. So, the party, it has a curfew. Five, no illegal activities, zero tolerance. This is where things get serious. Luxury yachts follow international maritime law which can be stricter than local laws. Anything illegal, drugs, risky behavior, or unauthorized activities can lead to immediate trip cancellation, being reported to authorities, permanent blacklisting from yacht charters. So yeah, this isn't the place to test limits. Four, no touching crew equipment. You might be curious. the control panels, navigation systems, jet skis, but touching anything without permission not allowed. These are highly sensitive and expensive systems and even a small mistake can cause major issues. Crew members are trained professionals. Let them handle it. Three, no overpacking. This one feels odd, but space matters even on a luxury yacht. Storage is limited and balance is critical for the vessel. That's why guests are usually advised to bring soft luggage only and pack light. So that giant suitcase, not yacht friendly. Two, no ignoring safety briefings. Before your trip starts, the crew gives a mandatory safety briefing. Most people don't pay attention, but on a yacht, this is serious. You'll learn about emergency exits, life jackets, evacuation procedures. Ignoring this, not an option. Your safety and everyone else's depends on it. One, no treating it like a hotel. This is the biggest mindset shift. A luxury yacht is not a hotel. It's a moving floating machine with crew coordination, weather dependency, fuel, logistics, route planning. You can't just demand things instantly or ignore schedules. Respect the system and you'll have the best experience of your life. Luxury experience section. emotional engagement. Now, here's the flip side. Once you understand and respect these rules, you unlock a level of luxury most people never experience. Imagine watching the sunset from a private deck, eating chef prepared meals tailored just for you, waking up in the middle of the ocean, jet skiing in crystal clear water. This isn't just a vacation, it's a completely different lifestyle. So yeah, a $10,000 per night yacht isn't just about luxury. It's about discipline, exclusivity, and a completely different world of living. And honestly, that's what makes it so special. If you enjoyed this inside look at the billionaire lifestyle, make sure to like this video comment, which will surprise you the most, and subscribe for more luxury and business content. Because trust me, this is just the beginning.

- Top 5 Old Motor Yachts For Liveaboard That Were Made To Last Forever: Kind: captions Language: en Picture this. You're sipping your morning coffee on the deck of your own yacht, watching the sunrise paint the harbor gold. No mortgage, no landlord, just you, the sea, and a vessel built like a tank that'll outlast most modern boats twice over. Today, we're diving into something special. Forget those temperamental fiberglass wonder from the early 2000s. We're talking about the workh horses, the legends. Five vintage motor yachts that were overbuilt, overengineered, and designed to cruise forever without breaking your bank account. These aren't just boats. They're floating homes with soul history and the kind of construction quality you simply can't find anymore. From Detroit diesel reliability to Taiwan craftsmanship that puts modern production to shame, we're counting down the top five old motor yachts that make perfect liverboards. Stick around because by the end of this video, you'll know exactly which vintage yacht could become your ticket to the cruising life. Let's get started. Coming in at number five, we have the Alban 43 Traler. And if you're looking for an affordable entry into full-time liverboard cruising, this Swedish design beauty might just be your perfect match. Let me take you back to the late '7s. Alban Marine was building a reputation for honest, non-nonsense trwers in Taiwan. The 43 wasn't trying to win beauty contests or break speed records. It was designed to be exactly what cruisers needed. A stable, comfortable, economical platform that you could trust offshore. What makes the Albin 43 special? It's all about that full displacement hole with a protected full keel. This boat was built to take a beating and keep going. We're talking solid fiberglass construction from an era when builders didn't cut corners. Here's what you need to know about the specs. This 43-footer sports a beam of about 14 1/2 ft. The majority of examples came equipped with twin Leman 120 or 135 diesels. And if you know anything about marine diesels, you know the Leman is practically bulletproof. These engines will cruise all day at 8 knots and push her to about 11 knots at the top end. Now, let's talk range. Stock, you're commonly looking at somewhere between 650 and 700 m, which is respectable for coastal and near offshore cruising. Can you extend that? Absolutely. Slow her down, manage your speed carefully, or add auxiliary tankage, and you've got even more flexibility. The interior layout is where the Albin really shines for liboards. Many examples feature comfortable two cabin arrangements that feel surprisingly spacious. You've got proper separation between living spaces, good storage, and layouts that actually make sense for full-time living. So, what's the damage? Here's the beautiful part. You can find late8s examples asking anywhere from $50,000 to $100,000. That's not a down payment. That's the whole boat. But here's the reality check. The Albin 43 is a coastal to near offshore cruiser, not an ocean crosser straight out of the box. You'll want a thorough survey, paying special attention to the deck core, teak, and windows. Look for evidence of diligent maintenance. These boats reward owners who take care of them and punish those who don't. Bottom line, the Albin 43 is your best entry-pric liabboard that still feels like a real ship. It's economical, livable, and backed by decades of proven service. At number four, we're stepping up to something truly special, the Chu Lee 46 LRC. And that LRC designation tells you everything you need to know. Long range cruiser. This boat was purpose-built to go the distance. Shoil has been building boats in Hong Kong since 1870. Yes, you heard that right, 1870. By the time they launched the 46 LRC in the mid70s, they had over a century of boat building knowledge baked into every hull. Many of these were built to Lloyd specifications, which means they met rigorous commercialgrade standards. The 46 LRC represents heavy displacement thinking at its finest. This is a boat that punches through seas rather than hopping over them, delivering that stable, confident ride that makes offshore passages manageable. Let's get into the numbers. She measures just under 46 ft with a beam of 14'8 in. Displacement comes in at a hefty 49,200 lb. That's a lot of boat. This weight isn't a liability. It's an asset that translates to seaeping and stability. Power typically comes from twin Ford Leman 120 diesels. Another legendary engine known for longevity and parts availability. You'll cruise comfortably at 8 to 9 knots. But here's where the LRC really earns its name. Fuel capacity. We're talking about 820 gallons of diesel combined with efficient displacement hole design. Published range figures hit 1,350 to 1,500 nautical miles at cruise speeds. That's truly go anywhere capability. Owners particularly love the centerline fiberglass fuel tanks found on many models. No iron tank corrosion to worry about. That's thoughtful engineering that pays dividends decades later. The layouts, many feature three stateateroom configurations, making them ideal for full-time cruisers who need space for guests or simply want that extra room. Quality teak joinery and wellplanned systems reflect the yard's heritage. Price-wise, there's significant variation. You might find a project boat for around $49,000 or a fully refitted example commanding 260 to 275,000. Condition and refit quality drive everything here. Survey carefully. These are 50-year-old boats, so expect some age related maintenance on laminates, teak, and systems. Verify any blister repairs or tank work thoroughly, but find a well-maintained example, and you've got a true longrange cruiser with respected heritage and proven economics. Number three brings us to Taiwan's golden age of yacht building, the Ocean Alexander Classico and Pilot House series. If you want quality everywhere you look, this is your boat. Ocean Alexander established itself as one of the premier brands coming out of Taiwan's booming yacht industry in the 80s and 90s. While other yards chased volume, Ocean Alexander focused on conservative engineering, solid fiberglass work, and finish quality that rivaled American builds at a fraction of the cost. The classicalico and pilot house variants spanning models like the 50, 456, and 486 became the gold standard for cruisers who wanted a boat that felt substantial. These weren't lightweight racers. They were serious cruising platforms with an emphasis on livability and systems redundancy. What makes them tick? Start with that beam of around 14t 8 in and a draft of about 4'2 in. Fuel capacity sits at approximately 700 g fed to twin diesels, often John Deere or Caterpillar units known for reliability. The twin engine configuration gives you redundancy and maneuverability, though it does mean double the maintenance compared to a single. But many owners feel that trade-off is worth it for the peace of mind. Where Ocean Alexander really separates itself is in the details. The joinery is exceptional. We're talking hand selected teak, thoughtful design, and hardware that's built to last. The pilot house layouts offer incredible ergonomics for long-distance cruising with excellent visibility and everything within reach. These boats cruise efficiently at 8 to 10 knots. And while they're not expedition trwler longrange vessels, the 700gal capacity gives you respectable range, especially if you slow down and let the displacement hole do its work. Let's talk investment. Mid90s, 456, 486, and 511 Classico models typically list between 240 and $370,000. Older 1980s 50-footers can come in lower depending on refit status. Yes, that's more than our previous entries, but you're buying into proven quality and strong resale value. The Ocean Alexander community is robust. Parts availability is good, and these boats age gracefully when properly maintained. Twin engines mean more upkeep, and you'll want to inspect systems carefully. But find a well sorted example, and you've got a yacht that'll serve you for decades. Bottom line, if you want that quality everywhere feeling in a pilot house trwler with proven Taiwan craftsmanship from the Glory years, the Ocean Alexander Classico series delivers. At number two, we're talking about a legend, the Defever 49. This is Arthur Defever's masterpiece, a full displacement cruiser that redefined what a twler could be. Arthur DeFor was a yacht designer who truly understood passage making. When he drew the 49, he wasn't thinking about marina cruising or weekend getaways. He was thinking about ocean crossings, extended cruising, and boats that could handle whatever the sea threw at them while sipping fuel. The DeFiver 49 embodies full displacement thinking taken to its logical conclusion. This hull form is optimized to jog along forever at about 8 knots, delivering ocean friendly motion and exceptional range that makes her a favorite among serious cruisers. The numbers tell the story. She measures just under 49 feet with a 15 foot beam and solid fiberglass construction throughout. But here's where it gets interesting. Fuel capacity ranges from 950 to 1,000 g. Yes, you read that correctly. 1,000 gallons of diesel. Combined with efficient displacement hole design, range commonly exceeds 1,000 nautical miles at twwler speeds. Slow down even more and you're looking at truly expedition level capability. Many owners report crossing oceans in these boats with fuel to spare. The raised pilot house layouts that became standard on many de fevers are absolutely ideal for live aboard cruising. You get 360° visibility, excellent weather protection, and layouts that separate public and private spaces beautifully. Systems are deliberately simple. No complex electronics or exotic materials to fail thousands of miles from the nearest yard. This is designed to be fixed anywhere engineering, which is exactly what longrange cruisers need. Now, let's address the price range because there's significant variation here. Project boats or older examples needing work can start as low as $34,000. Fully refitted turnkey examples push into the low to mid200,000 range. The spread reflects the wide variation in condition and the extent of refits undertaken. Here's what to watch for. Speed is limited. This is an 8 knot boat period. Some examples are twin screw, which adds maintenance complexity compared to single engine setups. and condition varies widely with age. Survey thoroughly, paying special attention to any age related issues. But find the right to Fever 49 and you've got one of the finest longrange trwers ever built. The combination of range, seaeping, simplicity, and proven performance makes this boat a perennial favorite among serious cruisers. And here we are. Number one, the Hatteris 53 motor yacht. If you want maximum live aboard space combined with that indestructible feel, this is your boat. Hatteris Yachts dominated American yacht building from the 60s through the 80s. When Jack Hatteris and his team designed the 53 motor yacht, they weren't messing around. This boat represents old school American construction at its absolute peak. Handlaid solid fiberglass hulls, heavy scantlings, and systems built to take abuse. Renowned marine surveyor David Pasco describes the 53 as a perfect blend of excellent quality and good design. The hull weighs approximately 55,000 lbs. That's massive even by today's standards. This isn't weight from poor design, it's weight from conservative engineering and substantial construction. The dimensions 53t 1 in overall with a 15t 10-in beam and about 4 feet of draft. She's big, solid, and feels like a small ship rather than a boat. Power typically comes from twin Detroit diesel 8V71 engines, either naturally aspirated or turbocharged. These are legendary power plants, the same engines that powered everything from commercial fishing boats to military vessels. Parts availability is exceptional, and mechanics who know Detroits can be found anywhere sailors gather. Fuel capacity sits at approximately 700 gallons, according to factory brochures. Now, here's the reality. Detroit diesels can be thirsty, especially if you're pushing hard. But cruise conservatively and you've got respectable range. The interior is where the 53 truly shines for live aboards. We're talking cavernous spaces with separate stateaterooms, proper galley facilities, and the kind of volume that makes full-time living genuinely comfortable. This feels like a floating apartment, not a cramped boat. What about pricing? You're looking at 1970s and 80s examples asking anywhere from $99,000 to $180,000. That's remarkable value for this much boat. But let's be honest about the trade-offs. That narrowish beam relative to length means she's not as wide as some competitors. Speed is modest unless you're willing to burn serious fuel. And those Detroit diesels, while bulletproof, do require maintenance and fuel. However, the massive parts ecosystem for Detroit diesels, the proven hole design, and the sheer space available make the Hatteris 53 the ultimate vintage leverboard motor yacht. This is a boat that was built to last forever, and many have done exactly that. So, there you have it. Five vintage motor yachts that were genuinely built to last. From the affordable Alban 43 to the commanding Hatteras 53, each brings something unique to the Live Aboard cruising life. The common thread, these boats were built during an era when quality mattered more than quarterly earnings, when engineers overbuilt rather than value engineered, and when good enough simply wasn't acceptable. - Interview with a superyacht captain: Kind: captions Language: en hi everybody my name is warren i'm the captain of a bird called motif dopamine yelling is my passion ybi crew [Laughter] [Music] i like charter boats because we have different guests coming on board we go to different places you have different people and personalities there's also a much higher energy level on the boat because we have to produce every day we can't get complacent with the owners you can't take your foot off the gas and you're always there to impress you know these charter guests are spending crazy amounts of money to come on board and they want the best and that's what we try give them so you get paid more money for it you have better itineraries you have more interaction with people and you can you get to show off your skills first of all i think it depends on where in the world you are if you're coming to the mediterranean guests are looking to be at cocktail parties and invite their friends over they want to go to cairns in monaco they want to go to san tropez it's all about being seen guests in the caribbean is a little bit more sports orientated they like beach setups they're like water sports diving fishing definitely more my kind of scene but of course we're in dual season boats so you're either in the mid or you're in the caribbean you know it depends on the age group as well if you've got a bunch of 30 year old crypto geniuses then they're all about drinking tequila from lunch time and having parties and throwing themselves off the top of the boat and then you have you know elder people and they wake up late and they go to bed early i mean there's a really huge array of reasons why a guest could be on a super yacht after all it's the ultimate escape so the preference sheets tell us where you want to go where you want to eat what you like to drink what you like what time you want to eat what kind of fruits do you want it also gives us an idea of the activities you want to do and then we also reference check a lot of the guests with other boats so captains will speak to each other and find out what the guests were like on the last boat what are the idiosyncrasies that they want us to remember that would help us to make it a smoother experience for the guests so tomorrow we're going out which was which is three days before the charter and we're taking out all the water sports gear we're pumping up the tubes and the trampolines and we're charging the sea bobs and the e-foils and we launching the tenders and we're getting the whole operation so everyone is is confident and looks smooth and looks professional when they're doing it there's nothing worse than arriving in a place and the guests all crowd around the back of the boat which they do as soon as you get somewhere and you're running around trying to get everything organized and the passeral out and the lines are all over the place so yeah a lot of practice yeah so i consider myself fortunate and unfortunate about 10 years ago um myself and my dad and my my girlfriend at the time we crashed in a helicopter and um i think everything changed that day our lives were turned upside down um i broke my my leg and my back and my hips and a whole bunch of other bones and the same for my dad and my my girlfriend at the time so that took me out of the industry for a long time you know i didn't walk for pretty much seven months and then when i could walk again i realized that i wasn't strong enough to work on the boats because you need to be able bodied you need to be energetic so i went back to university and i did a an mba a masters in business administration it was always my plan to do it because my dad had done it and following in in his footsteps was always part of my life so when i completed the mba i went into the corporate world and i worked in various different divisions in a company and it was really good i enjoyed it but um after three years it was three years and one day i walked into the ceo's office and i had a big smile on my face and i handed him my resignation and he said he was waiting for me to do that he could see that this wasn't uh wasn't going to be me forever so i was very luck very lucky you know i went to the mca and luckily i was working in a shipping division so they gave me my my licenses or revalidated everything and i came back and i joined the industry again and i had a second wind i was very excited and very happy to be doing what i loved again so i then got the last bit of c time i needed for my master 3000 which is your captain's ticket and it was a few years ago now that i passed that oral exam and i think i've never looked back since i'm right there in amongst everyone i very rarely ask someone to do something that i won't do myself or if i haven't done already you need to encourage the crew you need to show the crew what they might be doing right or wrong there's never one correct way to do something on a boat and i would prefer not to ever lead in that style and be like this is the only way you should do it it's best to let them make those mistakes if you can and then show them and show them a correct way to do it and if they show you a better way to do it even better i've had a couple of authoritative captains in the past and i was always scared to make mistakes and scared to ask a question and that's definitely not the style that i would like to run a boat by you know i like an interactive crew i want the crew to come and talk to me if something's wrong in order for crew to do a good job they need to have sufficient rest so we use different apps uh or different systems that we can monitor their rest you know it's very easy for the crew just to work on and on and on and you will see productivity go down very quickly so it's important that the heads of department and the captain monitor their crews work and rest periods so yeah there is definitely a trend for expedition boats at the moment and that's also because owners experienced owners want to go beyond they want to go further they want to go to new places most of us who've been working in the industry have been doing the med for example every year for 15 or 20 years so there's only so many places you can go to for example my owner considers a lot of the places here like gelato stops you stop at one town you pick up your gelato you buy a porcelain mug and you move off to the next place and he's quite an adventurous guy our guy he really wants to get out there he wants to go spear fishing and diving so for owners like that they are looking to go further we can do the maid for a couple of years in a row but then you want to go to the pacific or you want to go to greenland or you want to go to alaska or you want to go to the indian ocean you know maldives say charles mauritius madagascar these are amazing places to go to but that said don't forget more and more people are buying boats right now so there's not less boats coming to the mid in fact this is the busiest mid season that i can remember in history just to try and get a berth in a port or a marina is extremely difficult you know when we started we barely used yacht agents now yacht agencies are are huge businesses they have the relationships with all the marinas and the ports and they're the ones that can try and get us a berth and a location because if i try you know just off the bat there's a good chance that i'll be i'll be denied so yes there's more room for expedition and and to go further but i think the med's always going to be busy by far my favorite spots in the world to take the guests are the french polynesian islands you can just imagine it's deep oceans you've got four or five kilometers of sea and then um an atoll is basically an old volcano that used to come out of the water but it was weathered down over the years so you've got this ring of land which is only 100 meters wide covered by coconut trees and palm trees and then inside with the crater part it's just coral and fish and then this beautiful little land spiraling out from the inside and everything outside of that is just blue water as far as i can see it is just absolutely out of this world so favorite water sports toys i would say for me are the foil boards kite surfing foils although they're harder to use than normal kite surfing boards electric foils are a lot of fun when you get the hang of them but they're not always the best for guests because guests are not always water sports orientated and there is a risk of injury there so we do a little bit of training sessions and things like that and we have to keep a much closer eye on them there is a new jet ski that came out it's a 300 horsepower sea dude it was frighteningly fast i've never seen anything so fast like that on the water in my life i ride motorbikes and that thing would kick my motorbike's butts on a straight race so i guess they're just making things faster you know they didn't really come up with any new toys that we hadn't seen they had one interesting one it was like shaped like a u that someone had a remote control and they throw it in the water and you could remote control this little device to someone swimming in the water they can hold on to it and then you can bring them back to the boat so i mean i guess if the guests are lazy they can ask you to swim them around and you can just press the button and take them around the boat a couple of times thanks for recur for introducing me to ypi crew you guys are amazing lydia you've been a star thank you very much and i'm very happy to share our experiences with you day four of interview proof of life keeping your steps exactly it's exhausting stuff i'm parched we demand 15 million dollars for my imminent release unmarked bills ypi crew

- Hatteras - Why So Expensive? (2018-) Factory Video - By BoatTEST.com: Kind: captions Language: en the following opinions are solely those of boat Escom and its test captain hi caps t for boat s comment today we're at the Hatteras facility in New Bern North Carolina to discuss well the elephant in the room the higher cost of a Hatteras yacht the fact is there's more that goes into a Hatteras from many different aspects that command a premium value for a premium yacht for starters there's the resin infusion process it's much more involved and much more labor-intensive but the result is a lighter boat with more strength we'll also discuss how the factory maintains the quality control that makes the boat so well regarded with respects to their fit and finish let's start with the small component pieces and see how it comes together here's an infusion table this is where smaller items are built up the bulkhead salon decks etc the first step is to assemble everything dry with this de venise l foam it's cut so it can be bent and shaped Plus this allows the resin to seep well into the core also because each of the 1x1 cubes gets encapsulated if we were to put a screw into it water would still not get past a single piece that we screw it into the process begins with the foam core component laid on top is a fiberglass mat then peel ply then the media flow it all gets bagged a resin line is run through the bag then tack tape gets put onto the vacuum line which gets attached to the assembly now once the assembly is done then it's bagged and sealed everything here needs to be perfect because if flaws get in the whole piece is trashed there really is no room for error so it's a painstaking process and these suction tubes are then added to the bagging process finally the whole piece is sealed up tight and it's time to actually attach the hoses from a suction pump manifold to the bagged part as all the air is sucked out the bag forms to the part meanwhile our guy goes and fetches a bucket of resin adds hardener and mixes it up and now the clock is ticking when it's mixed put the pick-up line in release the clamp and the ongoing suction draws the resin into the vacuum bag and through the entire piece the result is a solid piece of material hard as a rock plus each piece gets numbered so they know exactly where it goes so is the process the same on a larger scale like when say a Yoda's resin infused yes yes it is here's a time-lapse on a 59 photos showing exactly that even the locations of the feed drums that hold the resin is carefully planned something on this level takes about 10 days of prep and the whole infusion process itself takes maybe 65 minutes to complete including areas like the bow thrusters straw its shaft logs even the hull side windows getting doubled up on the glass work but the point being the process is absolutely the same so how do they know they did it right well first visually but they need to go deeper than that pieces eventually have to be cut away a porthole a straw and exhaust port something like that they first look at it with a magnifier to ensure that it has the right number of layers that it's supposed to then that piece gets weighed and then put into a furnace the fiberglass doesn't burn but the present does so they burn it away and measure again now with some calculations they can determine the exact resident a glass ratio look up what it's supposed to be and ensure that they match but that resin infusion and quality control process is only one part of that added expense many more extra steps that had wrist aches are what not only add to the ounce expense but make the difference in a Hatteras for example the hala props are tuned to each other for better efficiency and the props are pitched right in-house in this prop shop the exact pitch of each prop is carefully measured against facts and if necessary moved over to have the pitch adjusted I've been on a lot of factory tours and this is the only time I've ever seen this procedure done rather than just putting a stock prop on the result is a smooth vibration free OTT even when observed where the vibration would be the worst right over the props now out of this in-house work leads to some very interesting technology being developed right here in the Hatteras plant for example the propeller shafts now have round anyways and the reason for that is when you take this round key way the load gets spread out all through the key way instead of something like this where we're used to seeing a square key way that would go into position here and as you put the propeller on you can see there's a little bit of a lift created at the end and that creates a stress riser right where the parklets lifted you have a torque stress created and you can see here it starts and it goes all the way around the shaft in a twist all the way around and down and it keeps going down and keeps going down until it ends right about here now it's at this point that this captain happy to shut this shaft off if he had kept it going then that's what you would have ended up with now because Hatteras went from the square keyways to the round key waste the shaft failures have dropped it to zero the rudder is not just welded to a shaft like we usually see instead the shaft is slotted and the rudder slides into that slot then it's welded so instead of the weld keeping the rudder from spinning on the shaft this weld only holds the rudder in place the slot keeps it from spinning so no loads are transferred to the weld now in this boat the fuel fill is on the port side it's kind of a neat setup let's take a look first of all we've got the patented Hatteras vents and you can hang a bucket under there to catch any fuel that might come out of the vents but that's not going to happen because we have fuel level indicators so that when it reaches 95% on the alarm goes off we silence the alarm and then we can slow the process down then shut it off when we get to the full point now all of this of course is connected to the Hat con app which is addressable on your phone so that you can be up at the fuel pump looking at the fuel fill and seeing how your level is take a look at this shaft strut bearing everybody has the grooves through here but we've all heard shaft squeal the way Hatteras gets around that is by cutting these annular grooves in to allow more water in to lubricate the shaft and eliminate the squeal even the paint job is not spared from the intense scrutiny of hatteras standards the first step is dewaxing in the home when it comes out of the mold it's got to be D waxed washed sanded and then stripped now it's important to note that I counted 10 to 20 workers on this boat and they're gonna be working for 10 to 15 days and that's just doing the prep work on this boat before getting painted so after all those steps you can see what the finished product looks like a beautiful glossy finish so clearly there's a lot more than meets the eye in a Hatteras there's a lot of quality control a lot of out-of-the-box thinking and a lot of unique engineering that makes these yachts among the best in the world premium price yesBut premium quality goes with it and that's our inside look at Hatteras yachts for boat Escom i'm captain steve we'll see you on the water [Applause]

South down to the Florida Keys where you can cut through the middle keys and then head north through the Florida Straits and Gulf of Mexico and then on the second route you would actually go north from Fort Lauderdale up to Stuart and then cross the state via the Okeechobee Waterway system this Waterway is controlled by a series of locks managed by the U.S Army Corps of Engineers to control the water level in Lake Okeechobee it is free to Transit through this Waterway you travel through the St Lucie River on the east side of the lake and the Colusa Hatchi River on the west side which comes out in Fort Myers both routes have similar distances however the route through the keys has less navigational restrictions since you do not have to deal with locks and bridges for the most part but you are more at Mercy of the weather and sea State conditions the owners of the boat decided to take the Lake Okeechobee route for this trip despite the potential hazards navigating through an area recently affected by Hurricane Ian the weather during this week was certainly not ideal for taking the Florida Keys route okay so how about we take a quick little tour of this Hatteras 64 Motor Yacht before we continue on she was commissioned in 2004 and she has a length overall of 64 foot 10 inches an 18 foot three beam and she draws four foot eight inches of water she has a fuel capacity of 1515 gallons of diesel and weighs approximately 116 000 pounds we will start out with the Machinery space that is accessed through a watertight door forward of the swim platform inside this door there is somewhat of a wet head for a crew member consisting of a toilet sink and handheld shower forward of this space the boat can either be fitted with an air-conditioned crew bunk or additional Machinery which is the case for this vessel you will see here two sea keeper gyro stabilizers and the fresh water management system the sea keeper gyro stabilizers consist of vacuum enclosed steel spheres that spin at speeds of up to 9750 RPM when the boat rolls the sea keeper tilts four and aft to produce powerful gyroscopic torque to counteract the boat's role moving forward through the next door you enter into the main engine compartment which houses two gen sets in sound Shields the fuel management system water maker all of the boat's air conditioning systems and of course the twin caterpillar engines each producing 1400 horsepower which totals 2 800 base horsepower combine these engines with the semi-displacement hull and the vessel can top out at around 28 knots and cruise comfortably at 18 to 21 knots get your checkbooks ready because fuel burn at Cruise will be somewhere around 80 gallons per hour and around 120 gallons per hour or more at wide open throttle up on the AFT deck you have a nice covered dining and seating area and engine room access beneath the stairs on the starboard side for when you are underway you can access the fly bridge via these teak treaded stairs or from inside a baft the lower Helm station the air-conditioned fly bridge with its natural teak deck is a great place for entertaining and hanging out while underway a capable tender rests aft on the boat deck and there's lots of comfortable seating and dining space a wet bar large TV mini fridge and a sound system that definitely shows that this boat is from South Florida I'm sure the retired owners from Indiana will fully utilize all of the subwoofers and high-end speakers for their outrageous parties at the helm you have two very comfortable helm chairs and even a retractable sunroof and - Hatteras Yachts - A Legacy of Innovation: Kind: captions Language: en you are looking at the bottom of a 53 port Hatteras motor yacht please maintenance-free molded fiberglass simple one-piece construction [Music] we've been building boats since 1959 the goal when Willis Lane was deciding he wanted a boat that could take the rough weather was to have a boat that looked like the local fishing boats have a little nicer combinations but be able to go out and to rough a weather and fish Willis Lane was an avid fisherman in the rough waters of North Carolina off of Cape Hatteras that's where he started looking at trying to find new materials and a way to build a better performing boat that could handle those rough waters his joke was if it's too rough for the wooden boat we should be able to go out there and fish those fish bite a little better with a little rough water [Applause] hatteras really wrote the script on large yacht production in the United States we showed so many others how to go that brings us to the G 265 Carolina an all-new boat to take address that next step to drive for a better performing boat a higher quality boat is this never-ending drive to do it better faster bigger this boat is going to use a infusion process where the stringers are infused with the hull in one shot so that now the stringers are not a secondary bonded in assembly that goes with this hull but rather it's all glass together one time we're really excited to see this boat when it's actually going to be in the tournaments when it's going to be fishing this boats going to be an amazing boat there always has to be a first and I don't think there's any first the larger than Hatteras was and showing away from boat building a yacht building we have to step back and say who we are and who we were and we are going to be

- JetStick 4® | An Explanation: Kind: captions Language: en jet stick has evolved over the past 30 years to become the most reliable and Powerful control system on the recreational boating market today it's like having an extra set of capable hands on board to help you get on and off the dock and Beyond when you're ready to go you simply Engage The Jets at the touchscreen put the boat in dock hold and you can leave the helm to go and untie the boat knowing the boat will not go anywhere when you return to the helm you simply start using the jet stick and move the boat boat away from the dock once you've gotten into Open Water you simply move the throttles forward and the boat automatically transitions into Power steer that mode that automatically derives a straight course based on your input no more over steering here from there you can start using the helm or you can go back to Power steer at any speed jet stick 4 switches the modes for you to get into docking mode you simply put the throttles into neutral and start using the jet stick accordingly if you need to use geost stationary lock to put your fenders out you simply hit the button and then when you return to maneuver the boat you just start using the jet stick and the mode switches automatically when you return to the dock engage dock hold and the system will hold the boat lightly against the dock while you go and tie it up you see jet stick 4 allows you to focus on your surroundings and your guests and not the controls themselves the auto switching at the helm is completely intuitive no need to disturb your guests or anybody else jet stick 4 is your companion for handling your hingley
